styles.name: Son Cubano
styles.description: The elegant, traditional roots of Casino.

styles.name: Documentary
styles.description: Films exploring Cuban dance history and culture.

levels.name: Foundations
levels.description: Core rhythm, body movement, and couple and solo mechanics

levels.name: Casino & Rueda
levels.description: Documentaries directly about Casino dance and Rueda de Casino

levels.description: Essential Cuban music documentaries providing cultural context

levels.name: Son, Rumba & Afro-Cuban

moves.name: Foundational Body Mechanics and Rhythm
moves.description:
Casino isn't just about the steps; it's about the flavor of Havana. The dance actually got its name from the **Club Casino Deportivo** where it all started in the late 50s. Casineros would say, 'Let’s dance like they do at the Casino,' and the rest is history!

Rueda de Casino relies on grounded Afro-Cuban body movement, characterized by bent knees and a pulsing rhythm rather than the rigid, upright posture of ballroom styles. The driving force is the "montuno," the syncopated rhythm of Cuban music.

moves.name: Solo Footwork
moves.description:
Foundational Casino footwork drills without a partner, building the rhythmic base (Guapea) and Afro-Cuban body movement. Rooted in the social clubs of 1950s Havana.

In Cuba, solo footwork is often called "suelta." It allows dancers to interpret the music individually, incorporating elements of rumba, afro, or mambo before reconnecting with a partner or the wheel.

videos.title: Beginners Cuban Salsa Steps Course - Class 1

moves.name: Partner Connection and Basic Steps in Couple
moves.description: Establishing the three-point lead-follow connection — lead's right hand on the follow's scapula, shared hand tension, and rhythmic push-pull. Covers the basic step in open and closed position.

moves.name: Core Casino Dynamics
moves.description:
This one actually translates to 'swagger' or 'looking tough.' It mimics the confident walk of a Cuban man on the street—it's all about that attitude!

Casino is defined by a continuous rotational dynamic. Dancers walk around each other exchanging places, keeping a low center of gravity and maintaining constant momentum without breaking the circular flow of the Rueda.

moves.name: Basic Turns and Elements
moves.description: Core turn vocabulary: Vuelta Derecha (right turn) and Vuelta Izquierda (left turn), plus essential elements like Enchufla and Dile Que No that form the building blocks of every Rueda call.

videos.title: Beginners Cuban Salsa Steps Course - Class 2 (Basic Turns)

moves.name: Dile Que No
moves.description:
Tell her no (The basic cross-body lead exit)

This is the fundamental 'rejection' move. You’re literally telling your partner 'no' to their forward momentum to reset the circle—it’s the cornerstone of almost every transition.

**Translation:** "Tell Her No"
This is the definitive transition move in Casino, used to close almost every pattern and return the couple to the open "guapea" position. It mechanically moves the follower from the leader's right side to their front, symbolizing a playful rejection.


*Translation: "Tell Her No"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Dile Que No ¡Díselo! (Improver)

moves.name: Dame
moves.description:
Give me (Switch partners)

**Translation:** "Give Me"
The fundamental partner-changing call of the Rueda. The leader releases their current partner, moves forward to the next follower in the circle, and immediately executes a "Dile Que No" to establish the new connection.


*Translation: "Give Me"*

*(No videos)*

moves.name: Dame Dos
moves.description:
Give me two. Give me (Switch partners)

**Translation:** "Give Me Two"
An extension of the basic "Dame" where the leader skips the immediate next follower and moves to the second one in the circle. It challenges the Rueda's spacing and timing as leaders must travel twice the distance before executing the "Dile Que No".


*Translation: "Give Me Two"*

moves.name: Enchufla
moves.description:
Plug it in

This name literally means 'Plug it in.' It comes from the visual of plugging in an electrical cord, perfectly describing that push-and-pull tension you feel between you and your partner.

**Translation:** "Plug In"
A fundamental Rueda turn where the leader and follower trade places, moving around each other in a tight, 180-degree semi-circle. It is named for the connecting and disconnecting action, reminiscent of plugging and unplugging an electrical cord.


*Translation: "Plug In"*

moves.name: Enchufla Doble
moves.description:
Double plug it in

**Translation:** "Double Plug In"
A variation of the standard Enchufla where the leader initiates the turn but instead of trading places immediately, they perform a back-and-forth feint. The couple only completes the full exchange of places on the second musical phrase.


*Translation: "Double Plug In"*

moves.name: Enchufla Con Mambo
moves.description:
Plug it in with Mambo

**Translation:** "Plug In with Mambo"
After completing the standard Enchufla, the caller instructs the Rueda to insert a brief "mambo" step—a syncopated, rhythmic break in place. This move showcases the Afro-Cuban roots of Casino by briefly breaking the circular flow for a percussive pause.


*Translation: "Plug In with Mambo"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Enchufla Con Mambo (Beginner)

moves.name: Vacilala
moves.description:
Check her out / Tease her

**Translation:** "Check Her Out" or "Enjoy Her"
The leader guides the follower into a free-spinning traveling turn while letting go of the hands entirely. It allows the leader to momentarily admire the follower's solo movement before reconnecting, echoing the playful courtship dynamics of traditional Cuban Son.


*Translation: "Check Her Out" or "Enjoy Her"*

moves.name: Sombrero
moves.description:
Hat

This is one of the more literal names—you’re basically making a 'hat' with your arms over your heads at the end!

**Translation:** "Hat"
A quintessential Casino move that ends with both the leader's and follower's hands draped elegantly over their own and each other's shoulders, resembling the donning of a large hat. It relies on a complex double handhold and fluid arm tracing.


*Translation: "Hat"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Básico - Cagua - Sombrero

moves.name: Exhibela
moves.description:
Show her off

**Translation:** "Show Her Off"
The leader uses a two-handed open hold to guide the follower through a series of inside turns, pushing them outward from the center of the Rueda. It acts as a spotlight moment, giving the follower space for styling and dramatic arm extensions.


*Translation: "Show Her Off"*

moves.name: Dile Que Si
moves.description:
Tell her yes (Closing the frame)

**Translation:** "Tell Her Yes"
The mirror opposite of "Dile Que No." Instead of transitioning to an open "guapea" position, the leader pulls the follower into a closed social dance frame. It is often used to reset the Rueda or transition into slower, more intimate step patterns.


*Translation: "Tell Her Yes"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Dile Que Si  (Improver)
Pasos de Casino Básico – Dile que Si
Module2: Dile que si, enchufla,  la prima, enchufla doble, enchufla con mambo at Rueda Club Munich

moves.name: Vacílense los dos
moves.description:
Both check each other out

**Translation:** "Check Each Other Out"
A variation of Vacilala where both the leader and the follower execute free-spinning solo turns simultaneously. It requires excellent spatial awareness to ensure the couple can smoothly reconnect on the beat after the independent rotation.


*Translation: "Check Each Other Out"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Básico – Vacílense los dos

moves.name: Dame Una Arriba
moves.description:
Give me one upstream (clockwise). Up / Forward

**Translation:** "Give Me One Up"
Instead of moving counter-clockwise to the next partner (the standard Rueda direction), the leader moves clockwise ("arriba" or "up" the wheel) to the previous follower. This call tests the Rueda's responsiveness by abruptly reversing the flow of the circle.


*Translation: "Give Me One Up"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Dame Una Arriba (Beginner)

moves.name: Enchufla Y Quedate
moves.description:
Plug it in and stay

**Translation:** "Plug In and Stay"
The couple performs the entry of an Enchufla but does not trade places or release the connection. Instead, they remain in a wrapped or side-by-side position, waiting for the caller's next instruction.


*Translation: "Plug In and Stay"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Enchufla y Quedate (Beginner)

moves.name: Dos Con Dos
moves.description:
Two with two

**Translation:** "Two With Two"
A structural Rueda call where the large circle temporarily breaks down into smaller sub-circles of two couples (four people) each. They execute synchronized moves within these mini-wheels before the caller reunites everyone into the main circle.


*Translation: "Two With Two"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Dos Con Dos (Beginner)

moves.name: Foto
moves.description:
Photo / Freeze. Photo / Pose

**Translation:** "Photo"
A playful, theatrical call where the entire Rueda freezes in a dramatic or silly pose on a specific musical beat. It emphasizes the social, game-like nature of Casino, often resulting in laughter before the dance resumes.


*Translation: "Photo"*

moves.name: Pelota Uno, Dos, Tres
moves.description:
Ball one, two, three

**Translation:** "Ball One, Two, Three"
The dancers perform a series of rhythmic claps or percussive body slaps (resembling bouncing a ball) while stepping in place. It highlights the importance of polyrhythm and musicality over complex turning patterns.


*Translation: "Ball One, Two, Three"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Pelota Uno, Dos, Tres (Beginner)

moves.name: Torniquete
moves.description:
Tourniquet / Turnstile

**Translation:** "Turnstile" or "Tourniquet"
The leader performs an Enchufla but maintains the handhold to keep the partner close, creating a tight winding or wrapped rotation. The motion mimics the mechanical action of a turnstile or the tightening of a medical tourniquet.


*Translation: "Turnstile" or "Tourniquet"*

moves.name: Aspirina
moves.description:
Aspirin

**Translation:** "Aspirin"
The caller directs the followers to perform a quick right turn (resembling an inside turn) on the spot while the leaders step back and provide the tension for the spin. The playful name suggests a cure for a "headache," perhaps referencing the dizziness of spinning.


*Translation: "Aspirin"*

videos.title: Aspirina, Cuban Salsa partner work, Rueda Club Munich with Sara and Auro

moves.name: Atrevido
moves.description:
Daring / Bold

**Translation:** "The Bold" or "The Daring"
A more complex variation in the Siete (or Panqué) family where the leader guides the follower into a left rotation using an unconventional hand hold on the follower's hip and shoulder. It requires a clear, assertive lead, hence its "daring" name.


*Translation: "The Bold" or "The Daring"*

moves.name: El Dos
moves.description:
The two

**Translation:** "The Two"
A call indicating a specific handhold or two-part movement. While variations exist, it often signals a double repetition of a basic turn pattern or a move where the couple rotates around each other completely twice before breaking apart.


*Translation: "The Two"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Básico – El Dos

moves.description: Plug it in and seat her

moves.name: Patin
moves.description: Skate

moves.name: Tarro
moves.description:
The name translates to "Horn" or "Cuckold". The move is rooted in Cuban humor, where the leader jokingly uses their hand to form a "horn" on the follower's (or their own) head. Mechanically, it usually involves an Enchufla or a Dame where the hand traces over the head during the partner exchange.

*Translation: "Horn" or "Cuckold"*

moves.name: Vamos Arriba, Vamos Abajo
moves.description: Let's go up / forward. Up / Forward

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Vamos Arriba, Vamos Abajo (Beginner)

moves.description: Family

moves.description: Ball festival. Ball festval

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Festival De Pelota (Beginner)

moves.name: Fly - Linea - Rolin
moves.description:
The terms translate to "Fly Ball - Line Drive - Grounder", which are Cuban baseball terms. Baseball is widely considered Cuba's national obsession, and these metaphors are deeply embedded in Cuban culture. The moves are a direct homage to the sport: 'Fly' mimics catching a high pop fly, 'Linea' mimics a sharp line drive, and 'Rolin' is a low scoop for a ground ball.

*Translation: "Fly Ball - Line Drive - Grounder"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Fly - Linea - Rolin (Beginner)

moves.name: Americano, Americana
moves.description:
The name translates to "American (Male), American (Female)". The move is a stylized sequence designed to gently mock or imitate the perceived rigid, linear social dancing style of Americans. To execute it, dancers adopt stiff postures or exaggerated, robotic arm movements for a few beats before returning to the fluid Cuban style.

*Translation: "American (Male), American (Female)"*

moves.name: Coca-Cola
moves.description:
The name translates directly to "Coca-Cola". The move is so named because the wrapping and unwrapping of the follower visually echoes the curves of the classic Coca-Cola glass bottle.

*Translation: "Coca-Cola"*

moves.name: Echeverria Con Vuelta
moves.description:
The move name translates to "Echeverria with Turn". José Antonio Echeverría was a prominent Cuban revolutionary. The three sharp foot swivels in the move are a tribute to Echeverría, representing the 'Three Minutes of Truth' from his famous final radio broadcast in 1957. Structurally, the move involves a cross-body lead variation culminating in a turn.

*Translation: "Echeverria with Turn"*

moves.name: Triple Play
moves.description:
Named after Cuba's beloved baseball — a three-count combination of claps and steps extending the Doble Play with a third accented sequence before returning to Guapea.

Continuing the baseball theme, this sequence involves three rapid partner exchanges or stylized "throws" around the circle.


*Translation: "Triple Play"*

moves.name: Doble Play
moves.description:
Another move from Cuba's baseball lexicon. The lead steps toward the center while the follow steps outward, followed by two rhythmic claps. The hand sign is a 'V' (two fingers).

Historically the lead clapped; modern variations often have the follow do them.


*Translation: "Double Play"*

moves.description:
Plug it in (The fundamental leader/follower place-switch turn)

An Enchufla incorporating a signature hand flip or styling attributed to the famous Cuban musician "Manolín, El Médico de la salsa."


*Translation: "Plug In with Manolin"*

moves.description: Directional calls — right then left — used especially in the Cubanito formation where all dancers face the center. Unlike Arriba/Abajo (counter/clockwise), these shift the group laterally while maintaining inward focus.

moves.name: Setenta (Basic 70)
moves.description:
Seventy. The foundational wrap move of the Setenta family.

This classic move is named after 70th Street (Calle 70) in the Miramar district of Havana, where the original Club Casino Deportivo was located.


*Translation: "Seventy"*

moves.name: Setenta Doble
moves.description: Seventy Two (72

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Setenta Doble

moves.name: Kentucky
moves.description:
This is a classic 'Miami-style' call from the 80s.

It’s actually a bit of a joke named after Kentucky Fried Chicken—a huge symbol of American culture for Cubans who had just moved to Florida.


*Translation: "Kentucky"*

moves.name: La Prima
moves.description:
The cousin (female).

In the early Havana club days, dancers used family names like 'Prima' (Cousin) or 'Hermana' (Sister) to keep things sounding respectable while switching partners in high-end social circles.


*Translation: "The Cousin"*

moves.name: Balsero
moves.description:
This name means 'Rafter' and emerged in the 90s.

That hand-waving motion you do actually mimics refugees on rafts waving down passing ships for help—it’s a powerful piece of history turned into a dance step.


*Translation: "Rafter"*

moves.name: Arriba de la Bola
moves.description: On top of the ball. Up / Forward

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Arriba de la Bola

moves.name: Cagua Complicado
moves.description: Complicated Cagua

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Cagua Complicado
Cagua complicado, Rueda Club, Cuban Salsa Partnerwork

moves.name: Cagua Doble
moves.description: A doubled Cagua — begins like a Sombrero with arms around each other's shoulders. The lead tucks under the follow's arm, the follow ducks and unwinds counter-clockwise. The Doble repeats the entire sequence a second time.

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Cagua Doble
Cagua doble at Rueda Club Munich

moves.name: Caza el Pato
moves.description: Hunt the duck

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Caza el Pato

moves.name: Coca Cola Doble
moves.description: Two consecutive Coca Cola left turns — the first on counts 5-6-7, the second on 7-1-2. More challenging than the single version, demanding precise lead timing across both turns.

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Coca Cola Doble

moves.name: Coca Cola por detrás
moves.description: Coca Cola from behind — the follow executes the signature left turn behind the lead's back. Develops advanced leading through shoulder and hip signals rather than hand cues alone.

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Coca Cola por detrás

moves.name: Dedo por Debajo
moves.description:
A variation of the 'Dedo' move. 'Por debajo' refers to leading the follower under the arm.

The 'Dedo' family of moves originated in the late 1980s in Havana, characterized by continuous circular motion and complex arm intertwining.


*Translation: "Finger from Below"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Dedo por Debajo

moves.name: Dedo, Guarapo y Bota
moves.description:
Finger, Cane Juice, and Throw Away.

A classic combination linking the arm wrap of "Dedo," a sweet styling moment ("Guarapo"), and a final toss away ("Bota").

Guarapo is a traditional, refreshing Cuban beverage made from freshly pressed sugarcane juice, typically served ice-cold (guarapo frío). It is a popular, inexpensive, and high-energy drink found at street-side stalls (guaraperas) across Cuba, often prepared using a, trapiche (small sugar cane press).

A Cuban named Esteban Pichardo recorded the word guarapo in the 19th century and defined it as a broth made from sugarcane juice extracted under pressure

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Dedo, Guarapo y Bota
Salsa variation - Dedo Guarapo y Bota

moves.name: El Beso
moves.description:
Translates to 'The Kiss'. This sequence typically brings the dancers into a close hold.

In the social clubs of Havana during the 1970s, callers integrated playful moves like El Beso to encourage brief moments of flirtation and closeness among partners, mimicking the intimacy of older Son dances.


*Translation: "The Kiss"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – El Beso

moves.name: El doce
moves.description: The twelve

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – El doce

moves.name: El Trompo
moves.description:
Translates to 'The Spinning Top'. A move characterized by rapid, continuous spinning of the follower.

Named after the popular childhood toy, it was introduced to emphasize the follower's balance and turning technique, particularly popularized during the 1990s timba explosion.


*Translation: "The Spinning Top"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – El Trompo

moves.name: Festival de Sácalas
moves.description:
A sequence stringing together multiple 'Sácala' (take her out) moves.

'Festivals' are a common way for Rueda callers to test the stamina and spatial awareness of the circle by repeating a foundational mechanic with increasing speed and varying orientations.


*Translation: "Festival of Take-Outs"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Festival de Sácalas

moves.name: Kentucky Complicado
moves.description:
An advanced variation of the classic Miami-style 'Kentucky' move, featuring additional hand tosses and turns.

As the original 'Kentucky' move (jokingly named after Kentucky Fried Chicken) spread, dancers in Miami and Europe added complexity to show off technical skill, resulting in this 'Complicado' version.


*Translation: "Complicated Kentucky"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Kentucky Complicado
Kentucky complicado at Rueda Club Munich

moves.name: La Holandesa
moves.description: The Dutch girl

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – La Holandesa

moves.name: Ochenta y cuatro
moves.description:
'Eighty-four'. An intricate wrap pattern belonging to the numbered family of moves that began with Setenta (70).

The numbering system originally referenced specific streets in Havana, but as Rueda de Casino expanded internationally, numbers like 84 were assigned arbitrarily to denote advanced, sequential variations.


*Translation: "Eighty-Four"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Ochenta y cuatro

moves.name: Ponle Sabor
moves.description: Translates to 'Put some flavor into it'.

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Ponle Sabor

moves.name: Setenta y Siéntala
moves.description: Seventy and seat her. Seventy (70)

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Setenta y Siéntala

moves.name: Siete con Coca Cola
moves.description:
Combines the 'Siete' (seven) foundational move with 'Coca Cola', which is a sequence of tight underarm turns for the follower.

The 'Coca Cola' turn sequence was popularized in Miami-style salsa and integrated into Casino. Its name playfully references the classic Coke bottle shape, an allusion to the follower's spinning silhouette.


*Translation: "Seven with Coca Cola"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Siete con Coca Cola

moves.name: Siete Loco
moves.description: Crazy seven. Seven (7)

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Siete Loco

moves.name: Siete Moderno
moves.description:
A contemporary variation of the foundational 'Siete' move.

As Rueda evolved in Europe and the Americas, older moves were updated with 'Moderno' tags to indicate a change in handholds or an added spin, keeping the dance dynamic.


*Translation: "Modern Seven"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Siete Moderno

moves.name: Three Way Stop
moves.description:
A modern, non-traditional Rueda call, referencing North American traffic signage.

This playful call originated outside of Cuba, likely in the US Rueda scene, where callers introduced recognizable English phrases and pantomimes into the dance to surprise participants.


*Translation: "Three Way Stop"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Three Way Stop

moves.name: Vacila el Tren
moves.description:
'Vacila' combined with 'el tren' (the train).

Refers to a sequence where dancers might briefly form a line or move in a locomotive-like fashion around the circle, a playful imitation of a train commonly seen in party animations.


*Translation: "Enjoy the Train"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio – Vacila el Tren

moves.name: Enchufla Moderno
moves.description: Modern plug it in. Plug it in (The fundamental leader/follower place-switch turn)

moves.name: Patin Doble
moves.description:
Translates to 'Double Skate'. It is an extended variation of the foundational 'Patin' movement.

As Rueda de Casino evolved to cover more floor space, callers extended the standard 'Patin' (skating) walking step into a 'Doble' pattern, challenging the dancers to maintain continuous gliding momentum.


*Translation: "Double Skate"*

moves.name: Vacilala con Columbia
moves.description:
Combines the 'Vacilala' turn for the follower with Columbia-style rumba footwork for the leader.

Columbia is traditionally a competitive, male-dominated style of Rumba from Matanzas. Integrating it into Casino demonstrates the deep Afro-Cuban roots of the dance and allows the leader to showcase technical prowess during the follower's free spin.


*Translation: "Check Her Out with Columbia"*

videos.title: Casino & Rueda de Casino -  Vacilala con Columbia (Intermediate)

moves.name: Paseo
moves.description:
Translates to 'Stroll' or 'Walk'. It is a relaxed promenade step used as a breather in Rueda.

Derived from the classic Son and Danzón walking steps of the early 20th century in Cuba, 'Paseo' was preserved in Casino to allow couples to reconnect and rest momentarily between complex, high-energy turning figures.


*Translation: "Stroll"*

videos.title: Casino & Rueda de Casino - From the archive -  Paseo (Intermediate)

moves.name: Camina - Dame Dos
moves.description:
A combination call linking a walking step ('Camina') directly into skipping the immediate next partner for the second one ('Dame Dos').

Callers use abrupt transitions like this to test the spatial awareness and reaction time of the Rueda. Mixing a relaxed walk with a rapid double-partner change creates a dynamic contrast in energy.


*Translation: "Walk - Give Me Two"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Camina - Dame Dos (Intermediate)

moves.name: Vacilala Y Ya Tu Sabes
moves.description:
Links the standard 'Vacilala' free spin to 'Ya Tu Sabes' (You Already Know), a placeholder for a local signature sequence.

'Ya Tu Sabes' acts as an insider code within specific local Rueda communities or schools. It fosters a sense of belonging, as only the regular members know which specific styling or pattern follows the Vacilala.


*Translation: "Check Her Out and You Already Know"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Vacilala Y Ya Tu Sabes (Intermediate)

moves.name: Cubanito - Media Derecha
moves.description:
A sequence pairing male solo styling ('Cubanito') with a sharp half-turn to the right ('Media Derecha').

Highlighting the leader's individual musicality is a core element of Cuban Casino. This move gives the leader a brief spotlight before rapidly re-engaging with the wheel's rotational flow.


*Translation: "Little Cuban Boy - Half Right"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Cubanito - Media Derecha (Intermediate)

moves.name: Prima Con La Vecina
moves.description:
Modifies the classic 'Prima' figure to include interaction with the neighboring couple ('La Vecina').

Moves that interact with neighboring couples exemplify the communal, game-like nature of Rueda de Casino, breaking the isolation of the individual couple and reinforcing the collective structure of the wheel.


*Translation: "Cousin with the Neighbor"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Prima Con La Vecina (Intermediate)

moves.name: Ya Tu Sabes Y Una Arriba
moves.description:
A custom local pattern ('Ya Tu Sabes') that resolves with the leader moving clockwise ('arriba') to the previous follower.

Combining a secret local move with an 'arriba' (upstream) partner change is designed to deliberately disorient dancers and create playful chaos, a hallmark of advanced Rueda calling.


*Translation: "You Already Know and One Up"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Ya Tu Sabes Y Una Arriba (Intermediate)

moves.name: Alarde
moves.description:
Translates to 'Boast' or 'Show Off'. It is a designated moment for exaggerated styling and posturing without a formal turn pattern.

In Afro-Cuban dance culture, 'alarde' refers to displaying one's confidence, skill, and personal flair. It traces back to the competitive machismo of Rumba Guaguancó and Columbia.


*Translation: "Boast" or "Show Off"*

moves.name: Lazo
moves.description:
Translates to 'Lasso'. The leader uses continuous overhead arm rotations to lead the follower.

The visual similarity to a cowboy's lasso gave this move its name. It reflects the playful incorporation of everyday visual metaphors into the Casino vocabulary during the 1970s and 80s.


*Translation: "Lasso"*

moves.name: Se Viro
moves.description:
Translates to 'It Flipped' or 'He/She Turned Around'. A sudden 180-degree pivot that reverses the Rueda's direction.

Abrupt directional changes are fundamental to the Rueda dynamic. 'Se Viro' acts as a reset button, testing the group's responsiveness and preventing the dancers from getting dizzy from continuous uni-directional turning.


*Translation: "It Flipped" or "He/She Turned Around"*

moves.name: Reina
moves.description:
Translates to 'Queen'. A move specifically designed to present the follower elegantly with a dignified posture.

In contrast to the fast, tangled knots of many Casino figures, 'Reina' slows the pace to honor the follower. It draws on the respectful, courtly presentation found in traditional Cuban Danzón.


*Translation: "Queen"*

moves.name: Ya Tu Sabes
moves.description:
Translates literally to 'You Already Know'. It is a modular call representing a specific, pre-agreed upon sequence unique to a local group.

The phrase itself is a common Cuban colloquialism. In Rueda, it functions as an inside joke, ensuring that only the core members of that specific group can successfully execute the mystery pattern.


*Translation: "You Already Know"*

videos.title: Ya Tu sabes Dame Ya Tu Sabes y Dame Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes y dame
Rueda de Casino - Ya Tu Sabes (Intermediate)

moves.name: Media Doble
moves.description:
Translates to 'Double Half'. The couple executes two rapid, consecutive half-turns.

The linguistic contradiction of a 'Double Half' is a classic example of Rueda humor. Mechanically, breaking a full rotation into two distinct halves allows for sharper, more syncopated hits on the musical accents.


*Translation: "Double Half"*

moves.name: Coca-Cola Con Puente y Arriba
moves.description:
A complex compound move linking a 'Coca-Cola' wrap, an underarm 'Puente' (bridge) pass, and a reverse-directional 'Arriba' partner change.

As Rueda callers sought to create longer, more intricate paragraphs of movement, they began chaining distinct elements like the winding Coca-Cola turn with structural transitions like Puente and Arriba to challenge advanced dancers.


*Translation: "Coca-Cola with Bridge and Up"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Coca-Cola Con Puente y Arriba (Intermediate)

moves.name: Familia Y Quedate
moves.description:
Translates to 'Family and Stay'. The entire Rueda joins hands in a large circle and bounces rhythmically in place.

'Familia' calls emphasize the communal aspect of Casino over individual partnerships. Adding 'Quedate' (Stay) focuses the group on shared polyrhythmic musicality rather than complex footwork or traveling.


*Translation: "Family and Stay"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Familia Y Quedate (Intermediate)

moves.name: Patin Para Arriba
moves.description:
Translates to 'Skate Upwards'. The synchronized 'Patin' (skating) movement is performed while the circle travels clockwise (arriba).

Traveling clockwise is inherently less natural for most Rueda dancers who are trained to flow counter-clockwise. Combining the challenging 'Patin' foot drag with the reversed 'Arriba' direction acts as a significant coordination test.


*Translation: "Skate Upwards"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Patin Para Arriba (Intermediate)

moves.name: Cambio De Pareja
moves.description:
Translates to 'Change Partner'. This is the foundational mechanic of Rueda de Casino, allowing the circular exchange of partners that defines the dance.

The origins of partner swapping in a circle can be traced back to French Contradanse and Spanish Danza, which evolved into Cuban Danzón and later Casino.


*Translation: "Change of Partner"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Cambio De Pareja (Intermediate)

moves.name: Enchufla y Cadeneta
moves.description:
Translates to 'Enchufla and Chain'. A classic Rueda move where couples perform an Enchufla and then link hands to form a continuous chain around the circle.

Cadeneta variations were highly popularized in the 1980s by Havana's Casino groups to emphasize group cohesion.


*Translation: "Plug In and Little Chain"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Enchufla y Cadeneta (Intermediate)

moves.name: Enchufla Y Cadeneta - Cambio De Direccion
moves.description:
A variation of the 'Enchufla y Cadeneta' where the caller signals a sudden change in the direction of the chain's rotation.

Reversing direction adds a layer of spatial awareness and is often used by callers to test the attention of the Rueda participants.


*Translation: "Plug In and Little Chain - Change of Direction"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Enchufla Y Cadeneta - Cambio De Direccion (Intermediate)

moves.name: Dame Arriba - Ya Tu Sabes
moves.description:
'Dame Arriba' means 'Give me above' (or next partner up), while 'Ya Tu Sabes' means 'Now you know'. It's a combination move requiring a partner change against the normal flow, followed by a deceptive fake-out step.

'Ya Tu Sabes' acts as a filler phrase in Cuban slang and became a staple fake-move call in late 1990s Rueda styles.


*Translation: "Give Me Up - You Already Know"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Dame Arriba - Ya Tu Sabes (Intermediate)

moves.name: Cero y Dame Arriba
moves.description:
'Cero' calls for dancers to perform a basic step in place or form a tight circle (zero), followed by 'Dame Arriba', a reverse-direction partner change.

The 'Cero' concept is often utilized to reset the timing of the Rueda before executing complex or counter-rotational partner changes.


*Translation: "Zero and Give Me Up"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Cero y Dame Arriba (Intermediate)

moves.name: Festival De Enchufla Complicado
moves.description:
'Festival' in Rueda signifies a sequence where the same foundational move (Enchufla) is repeated with increasing complexity or variations.

The 'Festival' calls became prominent in Miami-style Rueda (Rueda Miami) to keep dancers engaged through extended instrumental breaks.


*Translation: "Festival of Complicated Plug Ins"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino - Festival De Enchufla Complicado (Intermediate)

moves.name: Paseala (Led By Her Right, Left Hand)
moves.description:
'Paseala' means 'Walk her'. A stylistic leading technique where the leader walks the follower around them using a specific hand connection (leader's left to follower's right).

Specific hand-holds in Paseo variations allow for smoother transitions into open-break figures like Dile Que No.


*Translation: "Walk Her (Led By Her Right, Left Hand)"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Paseala (Led By Her Right, Left Hand) (Intermediate)

moves.name: Paseala Caminando
moves.description:
A variation of 'Paseala' where the couple walks together continuously (caminando), often traveling along the line of dance rather than staying in a tight slot.

This walking style is heavily influenced by the older Cuban Son, emphasizing a relaxed, elegant swagger.


*Translation: "Walk Her Walking"*

videos.title: Paseala Caminando & Vacilal y ya tu sabes

moves.name: Paseala y al Reves
moves.description:
'Walk her and in reverse'. The leader walks the follower and then abruptly reverses her direction.

Directional changes are key elements in Casino styling to match the syncopations and breaks in modern Timba music.


*Translation: "Walk Her and in Reverse"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Paseala y al Reves (Intermediate)

moves.name: Patin - Ya Tu Sabes
moves.description:
Combines the sliding 'Skate' (Patin) motion with the 'Ya Tu Sabes' fake-out.

Combining footwork-heavy moves like Patin with playful fakes is a hallmark of the spontaneous, competitive nature of street Rueda.


*Translation: "Skate - You Already Know"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Patin - Ya Tu Sabes (Intermediate)

moves.name: Saluda A Lo Moderno
moves.description:
'Greet in the modern style'. A stylistic move where dancers acknowledge their partners or the center of the Rueda with a contemporary flair or gesture.

'Moderno' calls often refer to integrations of hip-hop or reggaeton styling into traditional Casino moves, reflecting the evolution of Cuban popular music.


*Translation: "Greet in the Modern Way"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Saluda A Lo Moderno (Intermediate)

moves.name: Ya Tu Sabes Y Dame Dos
moves.description:
The fake-out 'Ya Tu Sabes' followed immediately by 'Dame Dos', skipping one partner to move to the second next.

Callers use this sequence to create intentional chaos and laughter in the circle, as dancers are tricked before having to sprint to the next partner.


*Translation: "You Already Know and Give Me Two"*

videos.title: Ya Tu sabes Dame Ya Tu Sabes y Dame Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes y dame
Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes y dame Ya Tu Sabes y Arriba  Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes
Rueda de Casino -  Ya Tu Sabes Y Dame Dos (Intermediate)

moves.name: Ya Tu Sabes Y Dame
moves.description:
The fake-out followed by a standard 'Dame' (partner change).

This is a fundamental timing exercise for intermediate Rueda groups, teaching dancers not to anticipate the partner change until the caller officially says 'Dame'.


*Translation: "You Already Know and Give Me"*

videos.title: Ya Tu sabes Dame Ya Tu Sabes y Dame Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes y dame
Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes y dame Ya Tu Sabes y Arriba  Dame dos Ya Tu Sabes
Rueda de Casino -  Ya Tu Sabes Y Dame (Intermediate)

moves.name: Vacilala y Dame - Ya Tu Sabes
moves.description:
The leader lets the follower turn freely (Vacilala), followed by a partner change (Dame), and immediately executing the 'Ya Tu Sabes' fake.

Sequences linking Vacilala and partner changes emphasize the visual disconnect between partners before reforming the circle.


*Translation: "Check Her Out and Give Me - You Already Know"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Vacilala y Dame - Ya Tu Sabes (Intermediate)

moves.name: Vamos Arriba - Un Tarro Sin Soltar
moves.description:
'Let's go up' combined with 'A Horn without letting go'. 'Tarro' typically involves a fake partner change (putting 'horns' on the partner), but 'Sin Soltar' means holding onto the hands.

'Tarro' moves involve playful deception. Performing it 'sin soltar' requires intricate arm twisting, highlighting the Afro-Cuban knotting techniques in Casino.


*Translation: "Let's Go Up - A Horn Without Letting Go"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Vamos Arriba - Un Tarro Sin Soltar (Intermediate)

moves.name: Vacilala Y Dame Ya Tu Sabes Y Una Arriba
moves.description:
A long, complex call chaining a free spin (Vacilala), a partner change (Dame), a fake (Ya Tu Sabes), and a reverse partner change (Una Arriba).

Extremely long calls are often memorized as 'choreographed' blocks by specific local dance groups to showcase their memory and synchronization.


*Translation: "Check Her Out and Give Me You Already Know and One Up"*

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Vacilala Y Dame Ya Tu Sabes Y Una Arriba (Intermediate)

moves.name: Un Tarro Sin Soltar - Hombres Abajo
moves.description:
'A horn without letting go - Men down'. Following the tangled arms of the Tarro, the men duck under the connected arms.

Level changes (Hombres Abajo) are visually striking in a Rueda and require strong core control, originating from the influence of Rumba Columbia in Casino.


*Translation: "A Horn Without Letting Go - Men Down"*

videos.title: un tarroo sin soltar = hombres abajo
Rueda de Casino -  Un Tarro Sin Soltar - Hombres Abajo (Intermediate)

moves.name: Abanico
moves.description: Fan

moves.name: Juana la Cubana
moves.description:
**Translation:** "Juana the Cuban Woman"
Named after the famous Latin song, this highly stylized sequence demands exaggerated hip movements and expressive, flirtatious footwork from the follower.

Juana the Cuban

This flashy move was named after the 1986 hit song by Fito Olivares. It’s meant to be just as flirtatious and high-energy as the character in the lyrics.


*Translation: "Juana the Cuban Woman"*

videos.title: Pasos de Casino Intermedio - Juana la Cubana
Juana la cubana at Rueda Club Munich

moves.description: Complicated fan. Fan

moves.description: For me, for you

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Por Mi Por Ti (Advanced)

moves.name: Bayamo
moves.description:
**Translation:** "Bayamo"
Named after the Cuban city known for its revolutionary history, this forceful move features sharp, marching steps and a commanding open frame.

Named after the city of Bayamo, the birthplace of Cuban independence and the national anthem. It’s a move that carries a lot of pride.


*Translation: "Bayamo"*

moves.name: Aguajea Para Abajo, Paseala P'Abajo, Adentro Y Afuera P'Abajo - Tarro
moves.description: Walk her around. Down / Backward

videos.title: Rueda de Casino -  Aguajea Para Abajo, Paseala P'Abajo, Adentro Y Afuera P'Abajo - Tarro (Advanced)

moves.name: Compota
moves.description:
**Translation:** "Compote"
A silky, mashed-together combination of fluid turns and body rolls, executed so smoothly it feels as soft as pureed fruit.

Compote / Carnival preserve

Named after the *comparsas* (carnival groups) of Cuba. A 'compota' is like a preserved fruit, but in Rueda, it's about the sweet, dense energy of the carnival!


*Translation: "Compote"*

moves.name: Retomando el Son Bailando Casino
moves.description: Así se vive el Son Cubano, raíz de nuestra identidad y patrimonio cultural. Ritmo, elegancia y sabor se unen en esta rueda donde cada paso cuenta una historia 🇨🇺

moves.name: Rueda De Son
moves.description: Rueda of Son

moves.name: La Salsa Cubana
moves.description: The definitive Casino documentary. Directed by Eric Joseph Johnson and Sarita Streng (2011). Follows a dance group in Cuba, traces Casino's origins in 1950s Havana social clubs, and features elder dancers recalling how Rueda de Casino first emerged. Award-winning festival film — premiered at the Chicago Latino Film Festival, won audience award at San Francisco Latino Film Festival.

moves.name: Rueda de Casino Documentary — DanceInTime
moves.description: Produced by Cuban Salsa School DanceInTime as a companion to 'La Rueda de Guanabacoa' by Sarita Streng and Eric Johnson. Focuses specifically on Rueda de Casino as a worldwide group dance phenomenon and its spread from Cuba to the global dance community.

moves.name: The History of Casino Rueda — Cesar Rojas
moves.description: Cesar Rojas gives a deep-dive interview on Casino Rueda history and its connections to salsa and other Latin dances. From the Ballroom Chat podcast (Love.Live.Dance). Discusses how Casino evolved from Son Cubano in the social clubs of Cuba, particularly Casino Deportivo de la Habana, before the Revolution.

videos.title: The History of Casino Rueda — Cesar Rojas

moves.name: Origins of the Casino Dance
moves.description: Traces Casino's influences from earlier dance forms — Contradanza, Danzón, Son, and even Rock and Roll. Highlights the Casino Deportivo as the birthplace where dancers would blend steps from various styles. Identifies Guaracha as the foundational musical genre for Casino dance.

videos.title: Origins of Casino Dance and its Musical Style

moves.name: El Casino Deportivo en 5 Minutos
moves.description: A brief look at what remains of the legendary Casino Deportivo club in Havana — the birthplace of Rueda de Casino. Shows the current residential area that replaced the original social club. Recorded by Alber Grau in 2016/2017.

moves.name: Buena Vista Social Club (1999)
moves.description: Oscar-nominated documentary by Wim Wenders. Follows Ry Cooder reuniting legendary Cuban folk musicians — many in their 80s and 90s — to record a Grammy-winning album in Havana. Includes concert footage from Amsterdam and Carnegie Hall. Selected for the US National Film Registry by the Library of Congress in 2020.

moves.name: Buena Vista Social Club — Adios
moves.description: Follow-up documentary revisiting the surviving original members of the Buena Vista Social Club. More reflective, exploring their contributions to Cuban cultural history. Features Omara Portuondo, Eliades Ochoa, Juan de Marcos González, and others.

moves.name: Eliades Ochoa — The Untold Story
moves.description: Chronicles the life and career of Eliades Ochoa, a poor country musician from Eastern Cuba who found international fame as an original member of the Buena Vista Social Club. Explores his lifelong dedication to preserving Cuba's rural Son tradition.

videos.title: Eliades Ochoa — Untold Story of Buena Vista Social Club

moves.name: A History of Cuban Dance
moves.description: Live-action 3D VR documentary directed by Lucy Walker, filmed on location in Cuba. Chronological journey from Afro-Cuban Santería rumba through tumba, mambo, cha-cha-chá, modern, salsa, breakdancing, and reggaeton. Features dancers from Danza Abierta and the Ballet de la Televisión Cubana. Premiered at Sundance 2016.

moves.name: Historia del Baile en Cuba
moves.description: Comprehensive Spanish-language documentary covering the full history of Cuban dance from the colonial era to present day. Covers Afro-Cuban Santería, rumba, mambo, cha-cha-chá, salsa, and reggaeton, revealing the broader history of Cuba through its dance forms.

videos.title: Historia del Baile en Cuba — Documental

moves.name: Discovering Cuban Son
moves.description: Explores Son's journey from the alleyways of Havana to international stages. Covers traditional instruments, fusion with other genres, and features interviews with Cuban musicians and dancers. Son is the direct ancestor of Casino — understanding it enriches Rueda practice.

moves.name: The Practice of Cuban Son — UNESCO
moves.description: Official UNESCO video for Son's inscription on the Representative List of the Intangible Cultural Heritage of Humanity (December 2025). Covers Son's origins in rural Eastern Cuba and its spread to Havana. Highlights singing, instruments, rhythm, and movement as inseparable elements.

moves.name: Evolución del Son Cubano
moves.description: Produced by Proyecto Retomando el Son (2021). Focuses on Son's evolution through three stages — from originators in Eastern Cuba, through the 1920s Havana movement with figures like Ignacio Piñeiro, to modern influences including songo, jazz, and funk.

moves.name: How Did Rumba Dance Develop in Cuba?
moves.description: In-depth historical journey examining the impact of African rhythms, Spanish colonial rule, and the transatlantic slave trade on Rumba's evolution. Covers its transformation from a street dance into a revered art form and explores its three distinct styles: Yambú, Guaguancó, and Columbia.

videos.title: How Did Rumba Dance Develop In Cuba?

moves.name: Roots of Rhythm
moves.description: Narrated by Harry Belafonte (1984). A three-part documentary exploring Latin music from its African and Spanish origins through Cuba. Directed by Howard Dratch and Gene Rosow. Traces the musical odyssey along slave routes, connecting Africa, Cuba, and Rio.

moves.name: Rumba All Stars — Legends of Afro-Cuban Drum and Dance
moves.description: Showcases a collective of elder and younger artists dedicated to preserving traditional Afro-Cuban Rumba. Founded and directed by Californian artist Miguelito León. Documents Rumba's origins in the ports of Matanzas and Havana in the late 19th century as an expression of freedom, community, and joy.

moves.name: Beyond Salsa — Timba Documentaries
moves.description: Multi-part documentary series from timba.com exploring the roots and history of Timba — the aggressive, dance-driven Cuban music that powers modern Casino dancing. Available as individual segments on the official Timba.com YouTube channel.

moves.name: El Bajo en la Timba Cubana — Feliciano Arango
moves.description: Explores Timba's origins through the experience of legendary Cuban bassist Feliciano Arango. A technical music perspective that helps dancers understand the rhythmic complexity driving modern Casino dance.

videos.title: El Bajo en la Timba Cubana

moves.name: TEAM CUBA — Que Suene la Timba
moves.description: 1999 documentary featuring members of Cuba's seven most popular dance music orchestras performing in a show that premiered in Havana and toured European cities. Captures the explosive energy of the 1990s Timba movement.

videos.title: TEAM CUBA — Que Suene La Timba
